mushroom_profile.summary: Leccinum Pseudoinsigne: check Birch, Aspen / poplar; Dry to slightly tacky, Dry cap; color varies by species; Whitish to grayish pores, Whitish to grayish or yellowish pores depending on species; and Scabers, Scabers on stem.
staining: Staining varies by species; slice and observe over 30 minutes
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
cap_surface: Dry cap; color varies by species
pore_color: Whitish to grayish or yellowish pores depending on species

records.edibility_detail: Use caution. Leccinum-style boletes can be hard to separate, and local ID matters.
records.field_identification: Check host association (Birch, Aspen / poplar), cap surface (Dry to slightly tacky, Dry cap; color varies by species), pore surface (Whitish to grayish pores, Whitish to grayish or yellowish pores depending on species), stem traits (Scabers, Scabers on stem), and staining reaction (Unknown, Staining varies by species; slice and observe over 30 minutes). Treat this as a comparison/ID record until image and local confirmation improve.

mushroom_profile.summary: Oak-associated leccinoid bolete with a dull, pitted/cracked brown cap, fine brown scabers, and bluish-green staining near cap margin or stem base.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

Look for an oak association, cracked or pitted brown cap, whitish-gray pore surface aging yellowish brown, fine longitudinal rows of brown scabers, and bluish/greenish staining near the margin or stem base.
stem_feature: Fine brown scabers in longitudinal rows

records.edibility_detail: Treat as a comparison/species-level Leccinum until confirmed. Leccinum identification is not beginner-simple; avoid relying on cap color alone.
records.mushroom_card_note: Oak-associated leccinoid bolete with a dull, pitted/cracked brown cap, fine brown scabers, and bluish-green staining near cap margin or stem base.
records.field_identification: Look for an oak association, cracked or pitted brown cap, whitish-gray pore surface aging yellowish brown, fine longitudinal rows of brown scabers, and bluish/greenish staining near the margin or stem base.

Use as a Leccinum comparison record: pore-bearing bolete with scabers on the stem. Confirm host tree, cap color, staining, and scaber color before naming.

records.edibility_detail: Leccinum species can be difficult and sometimes cause stomach upset even when considered edible; keep this one in review until better local documentation exists.
